text:
Long-horizon time series forecasting is a crucial but very challenging task that typically suffers from high computation cost and high forecast horizon volatility. In this work, we introduce a new method called Neural Hierarchical Interpolation for Time Series (NHITS) that aims to enhance the neural forecasting efficiency and accuracy for long-horizon tasks. Two key ideas are employed: (i) multi-rate data sampling (by designing sub-sampling layers that scale the receptive field in accordance with a compressed representation), and (ii) hierarchical interpolation (by splitting the input signal into separate bands and learning the forecast at each stage by fusing components in different frequency bands.

Abstract—we develop a theory showing that under some smoothness conditions hierarchical interpolation can emulate arbitrarily long horizons. Experimental validation on six large-scale datasets from the long-horizon literature strongly supports the efficacy of our model—namely the Non-Linear Hierarchical Interpolation Tree Spreadsheet model, (NHITS). Notably, on average, multivariate accuracy improvements over state-of-the-art Transformer based forecasting models are in excess of 20% while computational time reductions are in the range of 50×. Our primary model provides an interpretable, non-linear, and highly sparse decomposition of the underlying forecasts, which strongly argues in favor of its simplicity and robustness relative to existing large-scale forecasting systems.
explanation: The text shows plausible technical language but includes inconsistent acronyms ('NHITS' vs 'Non-Linear... Tree Spreadsheet model') and slightly jumbled phrasing, which suggests possible AI generation or heavy editing. Overall, the likelihood of AI authorship is moderate.
